Psalm 100 confronts us with a startling demand: joy is not optional. The psalmist does not invite us to praise God if we feel like it, or to worship Him when circumstances permit. He commands—with unmistakable force—that all the earth make a joyful noise to the LORD. This is not sentiment. This is not the luxury of the emotionally gifted. The command assumes that joy in God's presence is something we can do, something we *must* do, because it flows from the fundamental truth about who God is and who we are. When we stand before the God who made us, gladness is not an accompaniment to worship—it is the substance of it. We cannot truly serve Him while withholding our joy from Him.

What makes this demand possible is the radical claim of verse 3: we know that the LORD is God. Not that we theorize about Him. Not that we hope He exists. We *know*. And knowing this means understanding that He made us, that we belong to Him in the way sheep belong to their shepherd. This is either the most liberating or the most terrifying reality imaginable, depending on what you believe about God's character. The psalm assumes we know what kind of God He is—and that is why joy is possible. We are not the property of a tyrant. We are not servants of a distant, cold power. We belong to the LORD, and He tends us.

Verse 4 pulls us deeper still: we enter His gates with thanksgiving, His courts with praise. This is not reluctant submission. This is access. God has opened His courts to those He has made His own, and the appropriate response is not timid respect but deliberate gratitude and blessing. We are invited to call His name blessed. The final verse anchors everything: the LORD is good—not just powerful, not just demanding, but *good*—and His loving devotion endures forever. His faithfulness does not expire. It continues to all generations. This is the ground on which all the joy of Psalm 100 rests.

The force of this psalm is that you cannot truly know who God is and refuse to rejoice in Him. If you find yourself unable to worship with gladness, the problem is not your emotional capacity—it is your knowledge. You do not yet understand that the God who made you is good, that His devotion to you is steadfast, that you are held by faithfulness that will never fail. Psalm 100 calls you not to manufacture feelings but to see what is actually true and let that truth remake your heart. Make a joyful noise to the LORD—not because you are commanded to feel something false, but because everything true is worthy of your joy.
